title: Good Deflation Raises Real Wages Without Central Planning
body_html:
One of the strongest practical claims in the essay is that good deflation can raise real wages even without nominal wage growth. When better tools, deeper capital, and stronger competition improve worker productivity, wages do not need to explode in nominal terms for workers to become materially better off. What matters is purchasing power.

This is easy to miss in inflationary cultures because people are trained to focus on paycheck size rather than what the paycheck buys. But if housing, food, transport, energy, and consumer technology all become cheaper relative to income, living standards rise even if nominal salary moves only modestly.

Competition matters here too. Entrepreneurs competing for labor bid wages upward where productivity supports it, while competition among producers pushes prices downward. The combination is precisely what an ordinary household wants: better income opportunities and cheaper essentials.

The fiat fear of deflation often assumes the opposite because it looks at downturns caused by credit contraction. The essay insists that this confuses crisis effects with productivity effects. Good deflation is not labor oppression. It is one route by which capital deepening shows up in everyday life.
